ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific cold brew white coffee entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM (Coffee, with milk) is the closest defensible code for a milk-and-espresso cold brew beverage. No exact cold brew white coffee code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cold brew white coffee; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of cold brew white coffee is approximately one cup (250 ml / 250 g), as commonly served in Singaporean cafes and coffee chains.

nutrient_source.title: Cold Brew White Coffee — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cold Brew White Coffee (cr_id D09010024). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk and milk-based beverages — Atkinson et al., International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — full-cream milk (GI ~41)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cold brew white coffee in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 41 (low) based on the closest analogous food: full-cream milk with added sugar (GI ~41, University of Sydney GI Database). The beverage is predominantly milk-based with espresso and sugar; the milk matrix moderates glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (41 × 12.75) / 100 ≈ 5.2, rounded to 5.3 for a 250 g serving (5.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5 = 12.75 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.1 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 27 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request an unsweetened version of cold brew white coffee, as the 5.1 g/100 g sugar content in liquid form is associated with elevated blood pressure independent of sodium intake (AHA 2018); the low sodium (27 mg/100 g) is not a concern but does not offset the sugar risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.1 g sugar per 100 g and 0 g dietary fibre; liquid sugar absorbs rapidly causing post-prandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form, as 5.1 g/100 g of sugar with no dietary fibre in liquid form causes rapid post-prandial glucose elevation; an unsweetened cold brew with plain milk and no added sugar or syrup is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from full cream milk (estimated ~2.3 g/100 g) and added sugar (5.1 g/100 g) contribute to cardiovascular risk at regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit regular consumption of this sweetened full-cream milk coffee beverage, as the combination of saturated fat (~2.3 g/100 g) and added sugar (5.1 g/100 g) may adversely affect lipid profiles and endothelial function; switching to an unsweetened low-fat milk version is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from dairy and added sugar with no dietary fibre provide no anti-inflammatory benefit and may promote low-grade inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should opt for unsweetened cold brew black coffee to obtain polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory saturated fat and sugar load present in this full-cream sweetened version.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cold Brew White Coffee is a chilled coffee beverage made by combining cold-brewed or espresso-extracted coffee concentrate with steamed full cream milk and sugar. It is widely available in Singaporean cafes, coffee chains, and convenience stores, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or mid-morning drink. The beverage has a smooth, mildly sweet, and creamy flavour profile with a lower bitterness than traditional hot white coffee, owing to the cold extraction process which reduces acidic compounds. Per 100 g, it provides 62 kcal, 2.2 g protein, 3.6 g fat, 5.1 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 27 mg sodium. Calcium content is notable at 91 mg/100 g from the full cream milk component. The beverage qualifies as a sugar-sweetened drink (5.1 g sugar/100 g) and is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension in its standard sweetened form. The saturated fat from full cream milk (~2.3 g/100 g estimated) is a consideration for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. An unsweetened version with low-fat milk is a clinically preferable alternative for health-conscious consumers. The glycemic index is estimated at 41 (low) based on the milk matrix, but the absence of dietary fibre means glucose absorption is not significantly buffered.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010024) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary regional approved source for Singapore foods. Saturated fat estimated from full cream milk composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this single field is medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (full-cream milk with sugar, GI ~41).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.3 g/100 g) is estimated from the full cream milk component using USDA FoodData Central whole milk reference (SR Legacy #01077), as the HPB Foodo entry does not separately report saturated fat for this beverage. This field is medium confidence. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this prepared cold brew white coffee beverage are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry D09010024 as of 2026-04-16, and no supplementary data for these fields was found in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent prepared cold brew white coffee product: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Sources consulted and confirming unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo (D09010024, accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (accessed 2026-04-16). 3. Dietary fibre is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g) — this is expected for a liquid milk-coffee beverage with no fibre-containing ingredients. 4. GI value of 41 is an analogous estimate based on full-cream milk with sugar from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for cold brew white coffee exists in the database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (41 × 12.75) / 100 ≈ 5.3 g. 5. The input record listed GI as 0 with confidence 0.409 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this source is not on the approved list and the value of 0 is not defensible for a sugar-containing milk beverage; replaced with analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Added -1 justification phrase to notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document every -1 sentinel field by name, state the reason data is unavailable (not reported in HPB Foodo D09010024 or USDA FoodData Central for equivalent prepared product), and cite both sources consulted that confirmed unavailability. This resolves all g3_numeric_hygiene failures from the validator feedback.
